macro_segments.title: The Nature and Duration of Nidui and Nezifa: Minimum Periods and Regional Differences

micro_segments.title: Establishing the minimum period for nidui as thirty days
Nezifa as a lighter form of censure with a minimum of seven days, derived from the Miriam precedent
Rav Chisda's statement that Babylonian nidui equals Land of Israel nezifa, suggesting shorter duration in Bavel
Contextual discussion of rabbinic authority and cultural integration in Ashkenazi versus Sephardi communities

macro_segments.title: Self-Imposed Nezifa: Stories of Bar Kappara and Rabbi Chiya Responding to Rabbinic Censure

micro_segments.title: The difficult passage that prompted Bar Kappara and Rav Shimon to consult their father Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi
Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi's offense at Bar Kappara's question and Bar Kappara's self-imposed thirty-day nezifa
Rabbi Yehuda's decree prohibiting students from learning Torah in the marketplace and the value of privacy in Torah study
Rabbi Chiya teaching in the marketplace despite Rabbi's decree and his subsequent self-imposed nezifa for thirty days
Rabbinic debate on measuring the completion of nezifa and the principle of miktzat hayom k'kulo
The reconciliation between Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Chiya through divine providence and Rabbi Chiya's ultimate vindication

macro_segments.title: The Public Display of Torah and Intellectual Elitism: Tensions Between Accessibility and Proper Understanding

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chiya's citation of the verse about wisdom singing in the marketplace and Rabbi's correction using the principle of internal versus external wisdom
Historical context of debates over Torah translation and the difference between Jewish and Christian approaches to access
Rabbi Chiya's role in establishing widespread Torah knowledge through creating a public school system
Resolution of the marketplace debate through Rabbi Chiya's interpretation that acts of tzedakah should be private but Torah should be spread

macro_segments.title: The Story of Zutra bar Tuvia: Questioning Rabbinic Authority and the Limits of Deference

micro_segments.title: Zutra bar Tuvia's exegetical question about the final words of David and Rav Yehuda's refusal to answer
Zutra's realization of Rav Yehuda's offense and his self-imposed one-day nezifa

macro_segments.title: Digression on David: Self-Abnegation of Honor and the Power of the Righteous

micro_segments.title: Midrashic interpretation of the names of David's mighty men and David's practice of sitting on the ground in the study hall
The reversal when David assumed the role of head of the study hall after Ira HaYairi's death
God's reward to David for his self-abnegation: power to nullify divine decrees

macro_segments.title: Nidui for Personal Honor (Nidui Lichvodo): Limitations and Application by Non-Ordained Scholars

micro_segments.title: The principle that even a student can place someone in nidui if personally offended, though with limited scope of observance
The distinction between universal nidui (for matters of heaven) and personal nidui (for personal honor)
Rav Yosef's statement that a tzurba m'rabbanan may rule for himself in clear matters and the Rosh's concerns about this teaching

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Scholar with a Bad Reputation: Balancing Community Need and Moral Accountability

micro_segments.title: The dilemma of putting a needed rabbinic figure in nidui versus the desecration of God's name from inaction
Rabbi Yochanan's teaching that a rabbi must be like an angel of God; otherwise one should not seek Torah from him
Rav Yehuda's placement of the scholar in nidui despite his status, and the rabbis' reluctance to release him
Rav Shmuel bar Nachmani's intervention invoking the precedent of Rabbi's maidservant, preventing early release from nidui
The scholar's death by wasp sting and the question of his burial in the cave of judges rather than the cave of the pious
The exegesis of Rabbi Ilai's teaching about one whose yetzer hara overpowers him and the ethics of hiding sin versus public accountability

topical_tags.term: miktzat hayom k'kulo (part of day counts as whole)
tzaddik gozer (righteous person who nullifies divine decrees)
